title: Machina Arcana - A Look at 3rd Edition - The Books - Part Two
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F1hpeZ_T-ww

Part two of the 3rd-edition tour, and Daniel (@dungeondive) spends the whole video on the two lore-and-art books — the **Codex Arcanum** (covering 1st and 2nd edition material) and the brand-new **Codex Eternum** by Andy Lennon (covering *To Eternity*). His framing for the segment is that both books are *better made than the Kingdom Death Monster manual*, and the prose lives up to the production.

What they contain: the full scenario chapter texts blown up with their corresponding paintings, a bestiary section with the same heavy black-and-white line drawings Daniel singles out for praise, and a card gallery so the game's writing can be read outside of play. The Codex Eternum, in Daniel's read, works as a *standalone weird-fiction novella* — Andy Lennon's prose is rich enough that he could recommend the book to someone with no interest in board games at all.

The two unexpected highlights. First, a *making-of* section in the Codex Arcanum showing 2013 prototype sketches — item cards in a more traditional dungeon-crawl idiom, characters with abilities-to-upgrade that got cut during development. Daniel wants a whole book like this one section. Second, Uri Bilik has *named every single map tile*: the Courtyard, the Cross Pit, the Ritual of Chewed, Barrels of Fun, Blood Falls, Grizzly Grotto, the Incubator. Daniel emails Uri pitching a one-tile-per-scenario zine expansion.

----

Daniel's pitch — a zine expansion of one-tile-one-scenario mini-quests — would only really work because Uri *named* every tile. How often do you notice when a designer treats their map as a set of *places* rather than just terrain?
